title: Assessment of Neonatal Pulmonary Function
authors: Di Fiore JM, Travers CP, Carlo WA / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Assessment of Neonatal Pulmonary Function?
ทารกส่วนใหญ่ที่ต้องการ intensive care มา ด้วย respiratory signs — แต่ 'ปอดดี-ปอดไม่ดี' ไม่ใช่คำตอบที่ชัดเจนพอ. (1) Clinical signs (RR, retractions, grunting, cyanosis) เป็น indirect proxy ของ lung mechanics — รู้ mechanism → แปลสัญญาณได้ถูกต้อง; (2) Indices ต่างกัน: OI, a/A ratio, AaDO2, P/F ratio — แต่ละตัวบอกคนละมิติ; OI ต้องใช้ Paw → เหมาะสำหรับ ventilated patients; (3) SpO2 target range: 91–95% ใน preterm — SUPPORT trial (Carlo NEJM 2010) + NeOProM meta-analysis (Askie JAMA 2018) → กำหนด international guidelines; (4) Compliance + Resistance + Time constant สามารถ predict extubation success และ guide ventilator weaning — P-V loop ใน bedside ventilator บอก overdistention และ PEEP adequacy; (5) Prematurity → airflow limitation ยาวนาน: >50% ของทารกเกิด <26 wk มี abnormal spirometry เมื่อเป็น preadolescent — long-term sequelae เริ่มต้นจากการตัดสินใจ ventilation ใน NICU. เข้าใจ tools → ใช้ได้ถูกต้อง → minimize lung injury.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย 5 clinical signs ของ respiratory distress (RR, retractions, nasal flaring, grunting, cyanosis) — mechanism ของแต่ละอย่างและ clinical interpretation
คำนวณ alveolar gas equation, CaO2, และ 4 oxygenation indices (a/A ratio, AaDO2, P/F ratio, OI) พร้อมบอก direction ของการเปลี่ยนแปลงเมื่อ worsening
อธิบาย SpO2 target 91–95% สำหรับ VLBW: หลักฐาน SUPPORT + NeOProM, trade-off ระหว่าง lower (85–89%) vs higher (91–95%) target groups
อธิบาย pulse oximetry CCHD screening: sensitivity ~70–75%, specificity ~99.9%, cutoff <95% at >24h, lesions missed
อธิบาย preductal vs postductal sampling — รู้ว่าเจาะจากไหน บอกอะไร; hyperoxia-hyperventilation test แยก lung vs CHD vs PPHN
อธิบาย lung volumes: FRC, TGV, tidal volume, dead space, physiologic dead space/VT ratio 0.3 — ค่าปกติและการเปลี่ยนแปลงใน RDS
อธิบาย compliance (dynamic vs static, single vs multiple-occlusion), resistance (Mead-Whittenberger, equation of motion), time constant = R × C
อธิบาย forced expiratory maneuvers, FOT (forced oscillation technique), EIT (electric impedance tomography) — ข้อดี ข้อจำกัด
ประยุกต์ PFT ใน clinical: identify overdistention (P-V loop), PEEP titration, predict extubation success, pharmacologic effects (surfactant, albuterol, antenatal steroids)
ระบุ long-term pulmonary sequelae ของ prematurity: FEV1 deficit, BPD airflow limitation, airway reactivity — ตั้งแต่ neonatal period ถึง adulthood
1. Clinical Signs ของ Respiratory Distress
1.1 Respiratory Rate
Normal RR: 40–60 breaths/min; VT 6–7 mL/kg. อธิบาย optimal rate: minimize total work of breathing (elastic + resistive components)
ปรับ RR เพื่อลด work of breathing: RDS (stiff lungs, ↓ CL) → ↑ RR + ↓ VT (rapid shallow breathing); subglottic stenosis (↑ RL) → ↓ RR + ↑ VT (slow deep breathing)
Fig. 64.1 concept: normal 2 kg: RR 40–60, VT 12–14 mL; RDS: RR 70–90, VT 8–10 mL; subglottic stenosis: RR 20–30, VT 16–22 mL
Periodic breathing: first few months of life; chemoreceptor over-excitation → central apnea → short ventilatory cycles; distinguish from obstructed breaths (intermittent upper airway closure)
1.2 Retractions
เกิดจาก negative intrapleural pressure ที่ diaphragm สร้าง ต้านกับ stiff lungs + highly compliant chest wall ของทารก
ตำแหน่ง: substernal, subcostal, intercostal — ยิ่ง retractions ชัด ยิ่งบอกว่า lungs stiff หรือ CL ↓
Complications ที่ต้องคิด: airway obstruction, ETT misplacement, pneumothorax, atelectasis → ให้ assess ทันที
Improving sign: ↓ retractions with adequate inspiratory effort = lung compliance หรือ resistance กำลังดีขึ้น
1.3 Nasal Flaring
Dilation ของ alae nasi muscles → ↓ nasal resistance → ↓ work of breathing (newborns เป็น preferential nose breathers)
อาจพบโดยไม่มี signs อื่น: ระหว่าง feeding และ active sleep (begnin)
Genioglossus muscle: protudes tongue, maintains pharyngeal patency; laryngeal muscles regulate airflow during expiration — activation important for upper airway function
1.4 Grunting
Mechanism: vocal cord adduction ต้น expiration → elevated transpulmonary pressure at no-flow → ↑ lung volume → ↑ V/Q ratio; gas expelled against partially closed cords → audible grunt
Physiologic effect: maintain FRC + PaO2 equivalent to applying continuous distending pressure (same mechanism as CPAP)

Clinical Pearl: Intubation Abolishes Grunting → Must Apply PEEP
เมื่อใส่ ETT → laryngeal grunting mechanism ถูก bypass → FRC ล่มทันทีถ้าไม่มี PEEP. ดังนั้น maintenance of lung volume with PEEP (ปกติ 5–6 cmH₂O ใน term; 5–8 cmH₂O ใน preterm with RDS) เป็น mandatory หลัง intubation. Grunting infant ที่ยัง self-PEEP อยู่: consider CPAP ก่อน intubation; ถ้า intubate → adjust PEEP ทันที.
1.5 Cyanosis
Central cyanosis: ดูที่ลิ้นและ oral mucosa (ไม่ใช่ peripheral); ต้องมี desaturated Hb มากพอ (ขึ้นกับ total Hb)
Hemoglobin effect: anemia → PaO2 ต่ำ แต่ไม่ตรวจพบ cyanosis (↓ total Hb ← not enough desaturated Hb); polycythemia → cyanotic ทั้งที่ PaO2 ปกติ
Peripheral cyanosis: อาจ normal ในทารกแรกเกิด; แต่เกิดได้ใน ↓ cardiac output → assess capillary refill + perfusion
2. Blood Gas Measurements
2.1 Invasive Blood Gas — Alveolar Gas Equation

Alveolar Gas Equation
PAO₂ = PiO₂ − (PaCO₂/R) × [FiO₂ + (1−FiO₂)/R] ← full form; simplified for R≈1 and FiO₂+: PAO₂ ≈ PiO₂ − PaCO₂/0.8. PiO₂ = FiO₂ × (760−47) mm Hg at sea level. Mechanisms ที่ impair gas exchange: (1) V/Q mismatch (RDS → partial oxygenation; ↑ O₂ largely corrects it); (2) Shunt (congenital CHD, PPHN, atelectasis → O₂ ไม่ช่วย); (3) Hypoventilation (apnea → ↓ both O₂ + ↑ CO₂; O₂ ช่วย); (4) Diffusion limitation (rare cause ใน neonates). V/Q >1 (overventilation): ↑ PaO₂ slightly; ↑ dead space — CO₂ elimination inefficient.
2.2 Oxygen Content and Dissociation Curve
CaO₂ = (1.37 × Hb × SaO₂) + (0.003 × PaO₂) — 1.37 mL O₂/g Hb at 100% sat; 0.003 = solubility.
Example: PaO₂ 80 mmHg, SaO₂ 99%, Hb 15 g/dL → CaO₂ = (1.37×15×0.99) + (0.003×80) = 20.3 + 0.24 = 20.5 mL/dL; dissolved O₂ ~1% ของ total

Fetal hemoglobin (HbF): ↓ affinity for 2,3-DPG → O₂-Hb dissociation curve shifted LEFT → binds more O₂ at any PaO₂ < 100 mmHg — placental transfer advantage, but ↑ risk O₂ toxicity at high PaO₂ in preterm
Curve shifted RIGHT by: ↑ temp, ↑ PCO₂, ↓ pH, ↑ 2,3-DPG → ↓ O₂ affinity → release to tissues; reverse shifts curve LEFT (fetal Hb, alkalosis, hypothermia)

SpO₂ limitation: at SaO₂ >95%, impossible to estimate PaO₂ accurately (flat upper portion of S-curve) → PaO₂ may be 80 or 200+ mmHg with same saturation → must target SpO₂ 91–95% to stay on steep portion
2.3 PaCO₂ and CO₂ Physiology
CO₂ transport: primarily as bicarbonate (RBC/plasma); also dissolved + carbamino. CO₂ content-tension relationship almost LINEAR over physiologic range (unlike O₂)
Mechanisms impairing CO₂ elimination: (1) Hypoventilation; (2) V/Q mismatch; (3) Shunt; (4) ↑ Dead space (alveolar overdistention, gas trapping) — CO₂ diffusion rarely limited (high solubility)
PaCO₂ transition at birth: precipitous fall within minutes of first breath; PaO₂ rises quickly to 60–90 mmHg; some V/Q mismatch first day (intracardiac + pulmonary R→L shunting)
3. Oxygenation Indices
3.1 สี่ Index — Direction และ Formula
OI: corrects for mean airway pressure (Paw) — essential ใน ventilated patients เพราะ O₂ improvement อาจ reflect ↑ Paw ไม่ใช่ lung improvement; OI ≥40 = ECMO threshold
P/F ratio limitation: inaccurate when PaCO₂ varies markedly (doesn't correct for ventilatory contribution); use OI for ventilated infants ดีกว่า
Normal a/A ratio: ~0.9 at sea level room air; <0.15 = severely impaired oxygenation (typical ECMO trigger zone)
4. Noninvasive Blood Gas Monitoring
4.1 Pulse Oximetry: SpO₂ Target Range

SpO₂ Target ใน Preterm: หลักฐานจาก 5 Multinational RCTs (NeOProM)
5 trials randomized >5,000 extremely preterm infants: SUPPORT (Carlo NEJM 2010), BOOST II UK, BOOST II Aus, BOOST II NZ, COT. Lower target (85–89%) vs Higher (91–95%): Lower group: ↑ mortality at 18–24 months (RD=0.03, 95%CI 0.01–0.05; n=4,873) + ↑ NEC; Higher group: ↑ ROP receiving treatment; blindness + visual outcomes NOT different between groups. Death/major disability at 18–24 months: NO difference. NeOProM meta-analysis (Askie JAMA 2018;319:2190): confirmed these findings. Current recommendation: target SpO₂ 91–95% (low 90s) in preterm requiring supplemental O₂. Avoid SpO₂ >95% to prevent hyperoxemia (undetectable on flat dissociation curve plateau). Automated FiO₂ adjustment systems reduce time outside target range.
4.2 Pulse Oximetry: CCHD Screening
~25% ของ critical CHD ไม่มี symptoms ใน newborn nursery → discharge without diagnosis unless screened
Sensitivity: ~70–75%; Specificity + NPV: ~99.9%; cutoff: SpO₂ <95% at >24h
Lesions missed: obstructive left heart (HLHS, aortic stenosis, coarctation of aorta) — may have normal SpO₂ if no R→L shunt yet
Thangaratinam et al Lancet 2012;379:2459 + Mahle et al Circulation 2009;120:447 — established evidence base; widely adopted in US
4.3 Perfusion Index (PI)
PI: pulsatile signal / nonpulsatile signal ของ SpO₂ waveform — noninvasive tissue perfusion estimate
PI ≤1.24: predictive of high illness severity in newborns (De Felice et al Eur J Pediatr 2002;161:561)
4.4 End-Tidal CO₂ (ETCO₂) และ Transcutaneous CO₂ (TcCO₂)

ETCO₂: correlates well with ABG in term/preterm without significant lung disease; breath-by-breath display; ↑ usefulness for detecting rapid changes; underestimates true alveolar gas in small VT + high RR
Colorimetric ETCO₂: high diagnostic accuracy for ETT confirmation; positive = tracheal placement; negative = likely esophageal (false negative frequent at birth)
TcCO₂: superior to ETCO₂ in ill neonates; requires sensor preparation + location changes; provides continuous long-term monitoring with minimal blood sampling
Permissive hypercapnia via CPAP (Schmölzer et al BMJ 2013;347:f5980): CPAP vs conventional ventilation → RD −0.04 (95%CI −0.08 to 0.00) for death or BPD; NNT = 25
4.5 Preductal vs Postductal Assessment
Anatomy: ductus arteriosus enters aorta AFTER origin of right subclavian + right carotid → right radial artery = preductal; descending aorta (+ usually left hand/either foot) = postductal
PPHN: R→L shunting through PDA → preductal SpO₂ > postductal (>5% gradient = significant ductal shunting); foramen ovale shunting affects both sites equally
Method: two pulse oximeters simultaneously (right hand + left hand/foot) OR right radial + UAC ABG
4.6 Hyperoxia-Hyperventilation Test
Hyperoxia test: 100% O₂ for 5–10 min; lung disease → O₂ diffuses into poorly ventilated areas → ↑ PaO₂; persistent hypoxemia → R→L shunt
Hyperoxia + hyperventilation (PaCO₂ 25–30 mmHg): PPHN → more infants achieve PaO₂ >100 mmHg; structural CHD (fixed R→L shunt) → PaO₂ rarely >40–50 mmHg even with 100% O₂ + hyperventilation
5. Lung Volumes
5.1 Lung Volume Compartments

rows: Gas in lungs at end-expiration, in communication with airways
Total gas in thorax at end-expiration (includes trapped gas, e.g., PIE)
Max volume expired in one breath (crying VC in infants)

blocks.text: FRC measurement: helium dilution (in-communication gas only) หรือ nitrogen washout; TGV by body plethysmography (includes non-communicating gas → detects PIE)
RDS changes (Fig. 64.4): ↓ FRC (25 mL vs 80 mL normal); ↑ RR (70 vs 36/min); ↑ VD/VT (0.6 vs 0.3); ↓ CL (1.0 vs 4.4 mL/cmH₂O); ↑ RL; total work/min ↑ 5× (7770 vs 1440 g·cm/min)
FRC function: O₂ reservoir + buffer against large changes in alveolar gas tension between breaths — critical in preterm who have fast RR + irregular breathing
Dead space minimization: minimize apparatus dead space during PFT to prevent CO₂ rebreathing; VD/VT normally 0.3; increases with overdistention or gas trapping
6. Respiratory Mechanics
6.1 Compliance

Compliance = Volume / Pressure  |  Elastance = 1/Compliance
Dynamic compliance: measured during spontaneous breathing at zero-flow points (end-inspiration + end-expiration); requires esophageal balloon for transpulmonary pressure; underestimates at ↑ RR or ↑ resistance. Static compliance: airway occlusion during expiratory phase → complete pressure equilibration → divide exhaled volume by pressure change; no esophageal balloon needed. Multiple-interruption technique: multiple brief occlusions during expiration → compliance at different volumes → reveals overdistention (↓ CL at high pressures). Normal specific lung compliance: 1–2 mL/cmH₂O/kg (comparable to adult when normalized for body weight). Chest wall compliance: very HIGH in neonates (compliant chest) → retractions with minor CL decrease; chest wall collapses when diaphragm generates large negative pressure in RDS.
FRC effects on compliance: elasticity depends on lung volume → ↓ FRC → ↑ elastic recoil → ↓ compliance; specific compliance corrects for this
First 3–6 hours of life: FRC ↑ with clearance of lung fluid → compliance improves; fluid retention (TTN) delays this; variability in first 2h → explains transient ↑ RR in some newborns

Ventilator P-V loop: visualization reveals overdistention (S-shape, upper inflection) + compliance improvement; numerical compliance may show 'no change' post-surfactant if PIP not reduced (overdistention masks improvement)
6.2 Resistance
Resistance = Pressure / Flow — Conductance = 1/Resistance; airway resistance + viscous tissue resistance = total pulmonary resistance
Equation of motion: P = V/C + R·V̇ + I·V̈ (inertance negligible clinically → P = V/C + R·V̇); linear regression gives average R for breath
Mead-Whittenberger technique: R = ΔP/ΔFlow at midvolume point (peak flow) → ↑ value (highest resistance in breath); susceptible to turbulent flow
Multiple-interruption technique: R = pressure during occlusion / flow preceding occlusion → R at multiple volumes → partition airway vs tissue resistance

Laminar flow regimen: R constant throughout breath → techniques agree; turbulent flow: R flow-dependent → techniques diverge; flow limitation: ↑ pressure → no ↑ flow (common in lung disease + ventilated infants)
ETT leak: >10% → overestimates R, underestimates elastance; measure during expiratory phase with <10% leak for most reliable values
6.3 Time Constant
Time constant (τ) = Resistance × Compliance — duration for 63% equilibration of a step change; 5τ = 99% equilibration

Clinical relevance: ventilator inspiratory/expiratory times must allow adequate equilibration; short τ (RDS: ↓ CL) → needs shorter Ti; long τ (airway disease: ↑ RL) → longer Ti for filling/Te for emptying to prevent gas trapping

From flow-volume curve: τ = 1/slope of expiratory limb during passive exhalation; convexity toward volume axis = flow limitation (bronchoconstriction, laryngeal adduction, postinspiratory muscle activity)
Confounders: sleep state, posture, ETT size, FRC, gender, race, respiratory patterns → large intersubject + intrasubject variability in clinical setting
7. Advanced Techniques
7.1 Airflow Measurement Devices
Pneumotachometer (gold standard): all air must pass through it; in ventilated patients: attach to ETT with ≤10% leak; in spontaneous: nasal/oral mask tightly sealed; flow integrated → volume
Respiratory inductive plethysmography (RIP): noninvasive; estimate RR, timing, asynchrony, airflow, lung volumes; distinguish central vs obstructive apnea (with thermistor/cannula)
Hot-wire anemometer: improving accuracy + response time as alternative to pneumotachometer
7.2 Forced Expiratory Maneuvers
Thoracoabdominal compression jacket: inflated at end-lung inflation → rapid forced expiration; measures peak expiratory flow, FEV1 equivalent at FRC (V'maxFRC), forced deflation flow-volume curves

Flow-volume loops — patterns (Fig. 64.11): Normal = lemon-shaped; BPD = ski-slope (expiratory airflow limitation); subglottic stenosis = cigar-shaped (bi-directional limitation); tracheomalacia = crumpled loop; secretions = mountain-peaks
Long-term relevance: former preterm (esp. <26 wk) → expiratory flow limitation, abnormal spirometry as preadolescents; measures airway reactivity + BPD progression
7.3 Forced Oscillation Technique (FOT)

FOT: applies external oscillatory pressure/flow superimposed on spontaneous breathing or mechanically ventilated infant; measures respiratory system impedance (Z) = real (resistance) + imaginary (reactance) components
Advantages: noninvasive; no patient cooperation required; assesses both dissipative + energy-storing properties
Clinical data: Klinger et al Pediatr Res 2020;88:293 — FOT feasible in term neonates; TTN → ↑ resistance + ↓ reactance vs healthy term controls
7.4 Electric Impedance Tomography (EIT)
EIT: radiation-free, continuous, noninvasive; belt of electrodes around thorax; assesses regional ventilation + perfusion patterns
Tingay et al Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2019;199:384 — identified ventilation + perfusion defects in neonatal case report; more clinical studies needed
Potential applications: PEEP titration per region, detect uneven ventilation, guide high-frequency ventilation, monitor lung recruitment in real time
7.5 Work of Breathing
Work of breathing = ∫ P × dV (area inside P-V loop in Fig. 64.5)
Normal full-term infant: ~1440 g·cm/min; minimal at RR 30/min; Respiratory distress → ↑ up to 6× (8760 g·cm/min) — massive caloric expenditure → contributes to FTT in BPD
O₂ cost of breathing: neonates spend higher % of O₂ consumption on breathing than adults; distressed infants even higher → muscle fatigue → apnea → respiratory failure
8. Clinical Applications ของ Pulmonary Function Testing
8.1 Ventilator Management
P-V loop: identify excessive PIP → overdistention (S-curve upper inflection point), identify inadequate PEEP → alveolar collapse (↑ opening pressure at low volumes)
Distinguish alveolar collapse vs overdistention: both impair oxygenation but PEEP management opposite — EIT most promising for regional assessment
Extubation prediction: periods of ↑ CL + ↓ RL = window of best compliance + least resistance → ↑ extubation success (Bhutani & Sivieri Clin Perinatol 2001;28:487)
Surfactant effect: rapid ↑ dynamic compliance (P-V loop shifts left) → must ↓ PIP immediately or lung overdistention occurs even when numerical CL appears unchanged
8.2 Pharmacologic Assessment
Albuterol: ↓ pulmonary resistance in ventilated preterm babies (Raffay et al J Perinatol 2021;41:1704) — useful in BPD with bronchospasm
Antenatal steroids: ↑ lung compliance + FRC (response diminishes >7 days after therapy); improve lung maturation → ↓ RDS severity

Inhaled NO: no improvement in baseline mechanics; PPHN patients had maximal expiratory flows similar to ECMO (but still < controls); iNO to prevent BPD → ↓ bronchodilator need at 1 year (↑ airway reactivity protection)
Diuretics + methylxanthines: ↑ lung function short-term in BPD; improvements in R/CL do not always correlate with better oxygenation
8.3 Long-Term Pulmonary Sequelae

Preterm Lung Function — Lifetime Trajectory
BPD predictors: ↓ FRC + ↓ compliance + ↓ conductance early in NICU course predictive of BPD development (Back SA J Neurosci data applied clinically); gradual improvement as lungs grow. Without BPD: former preterm infants still have FEV1 deficits into childhood/adolescence; moderate-late preterm without BPD → pulmonary function abnormalities + wheezing + asthma. >50% born <26 wk have abnormal spirometry as preadolescents; many respond to bronchodilators. BPD longitudinal: persistent airflow obstruction into adulthood; some continue poor improvement long-term. Cardiorespiratory fitness: ↓ into adolescence/adulthood even without BPD. Right heart: ↑ pulmonary arterial resistance in BPD infancy; survivors at 8–12 years have comparable RV/LV function to controls after hypoxic challenge — partial normalization over years.
8.4 MAS and HFV Long-Term Data
MAS (meconium aspiration syndrome): alveolar hyperinflation + airway hyperreactivity to exercise at 7±2 years (longitudinal PFT)

HFO vs conventional ventilation: no pulmonary mechanics difference at 1-year follow-up in RCTs; BUT adolescents randomized to HFO had improved FEV1, FVC, peak flow, DLCO, impulse-oscillometric findings (Marlow et al Arch Dis Child 2017)
Hypoxemic RF with CDH/ECMO: ↑ risk of impaired pulmonary function long-term; CDH lung hypoplasia → chronic restriction + airflow obstruction
9. NICU Practical — Applying Pulmonary Function Assessment
9.1 Bedside Pulmonary Function Checklist
Clinical assessment: document RR, retraction score, nasal flaring, grunting, skin color every shift; integrate with SpO₂ trend + ventilator graphics
SpO₂ monitoring: target 91–95% in preterm on O₂; check probe site and waveform quality; perfusion index <1.24 → assess hemodynamics; CCHD screen at >24h (both hands/feet if CHD suspected)
Preductal vs postductal gradient: >5% preductal-postductal SpO₂ → suspect PPHN + ductal shunting; echocardiogram to confirm; start iNO protocol if PPHN confirmed
Ventilator P-V loop visualization: check after every surfactant dose → ↓ PIP if upper inflection point appears; check PEEP adequacy → low PEEP + opening pressure = need ↑ PEEP
ETT leak: minimize to <10%; use cuffed ETT or gentle neck pressure to reduce leak; resistance/elastance measurements most reliable during expiration with <10% leak
Time constant awareness: RDS (↓ CL) → short τ (~0.1 s) → shorter Ti adequate; BPD/airway disease (↑ RL) → long τ → need longer Te to prevent gas trapping → set I:E ≥ 1:3
Weaning readiness: CL ↑ trend + RL ↓ trend + ↓ OI + tolerating SIMV with low rate → trial extubation to CPAP/high-flow; avoid extubation when CL still falling
9.2 Long-Term Follow-Up Protocol
Inform families: preterm birth (even without BPD) → lifelong ↑ risk of asthma, airway hyperreactivity, ↓ exercise capacity
Pulmonary function testing in childhood: spirometry at 5–6 years old; consider bronchodilator response; refer to pediatric pulmonology if BPD or born <28 wk
Avoid tobacco smoke exposure (primary + secondary); monitor growth (weight gain → lung growth); seasonal RSV prophylaxis (palivizumab) in eligible preterm/BPD
10. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Grunting = Physiologic PEEP: Intubation Kills It
Grunting สร้าง self-PEEP ที่ equivalent กับ continuous distending pressure → maintain FRC + PaO₂. เมื่อ intubate → กลไกนี้หายไปทันที → ถ้าไม่ set PEEP ทันที → alveoli collapse → ↓ CL → ↑ PiP needed → volutrauma/atelectrauma. ดังนั้น: (1) พิจารณา CPAP ก่อน intubation ในทารกที่ grunt แต่ยัง hemodynamically stable; (2) ถ้า intubate → set PEEP ≥5 cmH₂O ทันที และ titrate ขึ้นตาม P-V loop + SpO₂; (3) document grunting resolution post-intubation as confirmation of adequate PEEP.

PEARL 2 — SpO₂ >95% ใน Preterm = Dangerous Blindspot
บน flat upper portion ของ Hb dissociation curve: SpO₂ 97% อาจ correspond กับ PaO₂ 80 หรือ 250 mmHg — indistinguishable by oximetry. PaO₂ สูง → ROP, BPD, oxidative lung injury. SUPPORT trial: higher SpO₂ target (91–95%) → ↑ ROP requiring treatment แต่ ↓ mortality vs lower target (85–89%). Optimal range: 91–95% — stay on steep portion of curve. Automated FiO₂ systems (closed-loop) ↓ time outside target range by 20–30%. Never target SpO₂ 97–100% ใน preterm on supplemental O₂.

PEARL 3 — OI ≥40 ≠ Immediate ECMO: ใช้ Trend ไม่ใช่ Single Value
OI = (Paw × FiO₂ × 100) / PaO₂ — เพิ่มขึ้น = worsening. OI ≥40 ถูกใช้ใน clinical trials เป็น ECMO threshold แต่ must be sustained (usually 4–6 hours) ไม่ใช่ isolated value. นอกจากนี้: OI depends on Paw — ถ้าเพิ่ง up MAP → OI อาจขึ้นชั่วคราว. ใช้ 3 values ในการตัดสิน: OI trend + CXR + echo (rule out PPHN vs CHD). iNO หรือ HFOV trial ก่อน ECMO ในกรณีที่ reversible cause (PPHN without fixed shunt).

PEARL 4 — P-V Loop After Surfactant: ↓ PIP ทันที หรือ Overdistend
Surfactant → rapid ↑ lung compliance ภายใน 15–30 นาที → ถ้า PIP คงเดิม → lung volume ↑ สูงมาก → overdistention → air leak (PIE, pneumothorax). Numerical dynamic compliance อาจดูเหมือน 'ไม่เปลี่ยน' เพราะมีทั้ง ↑ compliance ที่ low pressure และ overdistention ที่ high pressure หักล้างกัน. แต่ P-V loop visualization → upper inflection point ชัดเจน. ดังนั้น: ดู P-V loop หลัง surfactant ทุกครั้ง + ↓ PIP 2–4 cmH₂O ภายใน 30 นาทีถ้า SpO₂ ดีขึ้น.

PEARL 5 — Preterm Lung Disease เป็น Lifetime Diagnosis
ทารกที่เกิด <26 wk มีโอกาส >50% ที่จะมี abnormal spirometry เมื่อเป็น preadolescent แม้ไม่มี BPD. Moderate-late preterm (<34 wk) ก็มี peripheral airway obstruction measurable ด้วย impulse oscillometry. Mechanism: preterm birth interrupt alveolarization (GW 24–36) → fewer, larger alveoli → ↓ surface area → ↓ FRC, ↓ conductance; ↑ airway reactivity ตลอดชีวิต. Clinical implication: (1) family counseling ตั้งแต่ NICU; (2) referral pulmonology at school age; (3) avoid environmental exposures (smoke, pollution); (4) document baseline spirometry ก่อนให้ bronchodilators.
11. Evidence Update — Key Studies

Carlo WA et al (SUPPORT) N Engl J Med 2010;362:1959: 5 multicenter RCT; lower SpO₂ target 85–89% vs 91–95% in extremely preterm; lower → ↑ mortality; higher → ↑ ROP treated — defined modern SpO₂ targeting
Askie LM et al (NeOProM) JAMA 2018;319:2190: IPD meta-analysis 5 trials n=4,873; lower target → RD +0.03 mortality at 18–24 months + ↑ NEC; confirmed international recommendation for 91–95%
Thangaratinam S et al Lancet 2012;379:2459: CCHD pulse oximetry screening: sensitivity 69.6%, specificity 99.9%, NPV 99.9% using SpO₂ <95%; systematic review + meta-analysis across multiple cohorts

Mahle WT et al Circulation 2009;120:447: AHA/AAP scientific statement: pulse oximetry CCHD screening before discharge; ~25% CCHD discharged undetected without screening; established rationale for universal newborn screening
Schmölzer GM et al BMJ 2013;347:f5980: non-invasive vs invasive respiratory support at birth (systematic review + meta-analysis): CPAP → RD −0.04 for death or BPD (95%CI −0.08 to 0.00); NNT=25
De Felice C et al Eur J Pediatr 2002;161:561: perfusion index ≤1.24 predicts high illness severity in neonates — validated noninvasive perfusion marker from SpO₂ waveform
Mariani G et al J Pediatr 2007;150:418: pre- vs postductal SpO₂ in healthy term neonates after birth; preductal > postductal at 3, 4, 5, 10, 15 min postnatal; described normal transition physiology

Bhutani VK, Sivieri EM Clin Perinatol 2001;28:487: clinical use of pulmonary mechanics + waveform graphics in neonates — comprehensive reference for compliance/resistance-guided ventilation and extubation readiness

Peterson-Carmichael S et al Ann Am Thorac Soc 2016;13:S1: ATS/ERS official workshop report: evaluation of respiratory mechanics + function in pediatric + neonatal ICUs — standardized measurement methods
Klinger AP et al Pediatr Res 2020;88:293: FOT to quantify respiratory mechanics in term neonates; TTN → ↑ resistance + ↓ reactance vs healthy term controls; feasibility established

Raffay TM et al J Perinatol 2021;41:1704: response to first-dose inhaled albuterol in mechanically ventilated preterm infants; ↓ pulmonary resistance — supports bronchodilator use in BPD with bronchospasm

Tingay DG et al Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2019;199:384: EIT identifies regional ventilation + perfusion defects in neonatal case — proof-of-concept for clinical use in guiding regional lung management

Tepper RS et al J Pediatr 1986;109:1040: expiratory flow limitation in BPD infants — early characterization of 'ski-slope' flow-volume loop; established basis for long-term airway reactivity monitoring
Askie LM et al N Engl J Med 2003;349:959: BOOST trial pilot; oxygen saturation targets and outcomes in extremely preterm; early RCT data contributing to NeOProM individual patient data synthesis

Back SA et al J Neurosci 2001;21:1302: (cross-reference from Ch53): preoligodendrocyte vulnerability window GW 23–32 — same period when FRC instability + frequent desaturations → lung function protects brain
Fischer JB et al Pediatr Pulmonol 1988;5:10: identifying lung overdistention by volume-pressure loops during mechanical ventilation — original description of P-V loop clinical application in NICU

Blanken MO et al N Engl J Med 2013;368:1791: RSV and recurrent wheeze in healthy preterm infants — respiratory syncytial virus contributes to ↑ airway reactivity in preterm population independent of BPD
12. Self-Check Q&A
Q: Grunting ช่วย maintain oxygenation ได้อย่างไร และ clinical implication เมื่อต้อง intubate?

ตอบ: Mechanism: vocal cord adduction ต้น expiration → trapped gas → ↑ transpulmonary pressure at no-flow → ↑ lung volume → ↑ V/Q → ↑ PaO₂; audible grunt = gas expelled against partially closed cords. Equivalent physiologic effect = CPAP (continuous distending pressure). Intubation: abolishes grunting → mandatory PEEP immediately (≥5 cmH₂O preterm, ≥4–5 term); without PEEP → alveolar collapse → ↓ CL → ↑ work → volutrauma; consider CPAP trial before intubation in grunting infant who is otherwise stable.
Q: คำนวณ OI และบอกว่า OI บอกอะไรที่ P/F ratio บอกไม่ได้

ตอบ: OI = (Paw × FiO₂ × 100) / PaO₂. Example: Paw 14 cmH₂O, FiO₂ 0.8, PaO₂ 50 mmHg → OI = (14 × 0.8 × 100)/50 = 22.4. ข้อดีของ OI vs P/F: OI incorporates Paw — corrects for ventilatory contribution to oxygenation; P/F = PaO₂/FiO₂ แต่ถ้า Paw สูงมาก → PaO₂ ดีขึ้น โดยไม่ได้ lung improve → P/F misleadingly normal; OI ↑ ถ้า Paw ↑ → honest reflection of severity. OI ≥40 sustained 4–6h = conventional ECMO threshold.
Q: SpO₂ target 91–95% ใน preterm: หลักฐานจาก SUPPORT/NeOProM คืออะไร และ trade-off ระหว่าง 2 groups?

ตอบ: SUPPORT (Carlo NEJM 2010) + 4 BOOST trials → NeOProM IPD meta-analysis (Askie JAMA 2018;319:2190): lower target (85–89%) → ↑ mortality (RD +0.03, 95%CI 0.01–0.05) + ↑ NEC; higher target (91–95%) → ↑ ROP receiving treatment (but blindness + vision outcomes NOT different; composite death/disability NOT different). Recommendation: target 91–95% — avoid >95% (PaO₂ unmeasurable; hyperoxemia); avoid <90% (mortality risk). Automated FiO₂ → ↓ excursions outside target.
Q: Dynamic vs static compliance: ต่างกันอย่างไร และ เมื่อไหร่ใช้ static?

ตอบ: Dynamic: measured at zero-flow points during spontaneous breathing (ΔV/ΔP_esophageal); usable bedside without special intervention; underestimates true CL when ↑ RR or ↑ RL (pressure not equilibrated at zero-flow). Static: airway occlusion during expiration → complete pressure equilibration; divide exhaled volume by ΔP (no esophageal balloon needed); more accurate; multiple-interruption technique: occlusions at multiple volumes → reveals if compliance constant or changes with volume (identifies overdistention at high PIP).
Q: Time constant ในทางคลินิก: หมายถึงอะไร และ ทำไม BPD กับ RDS ต้องตั้งเครื่องต่างกัน?

ตอบ: τ = R × C: duration for 63% equilibration; 5τ = 99% equilibration (practical complete equilibration). RDS: ↓ CL → short τ (~0.05–0.15 s) → short Ti adequate (0.3 s Ti ≈ 2–3τ); BPD/airway disease: ↑ RL → long τ (~0.3–0.7 s) → need longer Te (↑ I:E to 1:3 or 1:4) to allow full exhalation; if Te < 3τ → gas trapping → ↑ auto-PEEP → alveolar overdistention + ↓ VT → CO₂ retention → may look like 'need ↑ PIP' when actually need ↑ expiratory time.
Q: Preductal vs postductal SpO₂: anatomy, interpretation, PPHN vs CHD

ตอบ: Anatomy: ductus arteriosus enters aorta AFTER right subclavian + right carotid origins → right hand = preductal; left hand/feet = postductal. R→L ductal shunting: preductal SpO₂ > postductal SpO₂ (>5% gap = clinically significant). PPHN: R→L shunt via PDA (ductal) + foramen ovale (affects both equally); echo confirms. Hyperoxia-hyperventilation test: PaCO₂ 25–30 mmHg + FiO₂ 1.0 → PPHN → PaO₂ often >100 mmHg; structural CHD (fixed shunt) → PaO₂ rarely >40–50 mmHg.
Q: CCHD pulse oximetry screening: sensitivity, specificity, cutoff, lesions missed

ตอบ: Timing: >24 hours after birth; cutoff SpO₂ <95% in either hand or foot. Performance: sensitivity ~70–75% (Thangaratinam Lancet 2012); specificity + NPV ~99.9%. Lesions most likely missed: obstructive LEFT heart lesions (HLHS, critical aortic stenosis, coarctation of aorta) — may have no R→L shunt yet → SpO₂ normal. Why screen? ~25% CCHD not symptomatic at birth + discharged without diagnosis → screening catches them before ductal closure → earlier surgical intervention.
Q: P-V loop: อ่าน overdistention, alveolar collapse, และ surfactant response อย่างไร?

ตอบ: Upper inflection point (UIP): flat upper portion of P-V loop = overdistention; PIP too high → ↓ PIP. Lower inflection point (LIP): flat lower portion = alveolar collapse; high PEEP needed to recruit. Optimal PEEP: ≥LIP (on deflation limb if possible); avoid UIP. Surfactant: loop shifts LEFT (↓ opening pressure needed); if PIP unchanged → operating above UIP → overdistention; numerical CL unchanged (masked by both ↑ at low P + overdistention at high P); must visualize loop + ↓ PIP 2–4 cmH₂O within 30 min post-dose.
Q: Long-term pulmonary outcomes ของ preterm: ต้องบอกครอบครัวอะไรบ้าง?

ตอบ: Without BPD: FEV1 deficits into childhood/adolescence; peripheral airway obstruction measurable; moderate-late preterm → wheezing + asthma. With BPD: persistent expiratory flow limitation; >50% born <26 wk → abnormal spirometry as preadolescents; many respond to bronchodilators; some have persistent BPD into adulthood with poor spirometry trajectory. Cardiorespiratory fitness: ↓ into adolescence/adulthood. Family counseling: (1) avoid smoke exposure; (2) pulmonology follow-up at school age; (3) spirometry at 5–6 years; (4) RSV prophylaxis eligible years; (5) indoor air quality awareness.
Q: Flow-volume loop shapes: match pattern to diagnosis

ตอบ: Normal: lemon-shaped. BPD: ski-slope loop (expiratory airflow limitation → scoop-out of expiratory curve). Subglottic stenosis: cigar-shaped (both inspiratory + expiratory limitation). Intraluminal carina obstruction/aberrant vessel: bun-shaped (intrathoracic inspiratory limitation). Tracheomalacia: crumpled loop (unstable airway, variable pattern). Secretions: mountain-peaks (erratic, spiky pattern). These loops from forced expiratory maneuver + RIP/pneumotachograph — each pattern directs specific diagnostic/therapeutic action.

Day 162 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.65
บทต่อไป: Chapter 65 — Respiratory Distress Syndrome. เตรียมตัว: RDS pathophysiology — surfactant deficiency → ↑ surface tension → alveolar collapse → progressive atelectasis; V/Q mismatch + R→L shunting; antenatal corticosteroids: betamethasone vs dexamethasone, timing, rescue courses; surfactant therapy: natural vs synthetic, prophylactic vs rescue, INSURE vs LISA/MIST techniques, Less Invasive Surfactant Administration (LISA); CPAP vs intubation-surfactant-extubation: SUPPORT, COIN, CURPAP trials; techniques for ABG sampling in RDS; radiographic findings; BPD prevention strategies.
